item.title: Thanks for 150
item.content:encoded: I've had quite the journey this year and it really began with putting in the work on 5k con and creating this piece. Thank you for +150 overall rating, and I look forward to more stories soon.

item.title: A lot of heart
item.content:encoded:
This one really hit me on an emotional level. It’s got an intimacy that I don’t normally expect from the wiki. And the, as you put it, exploration of grief was well done. I was surprised at the level of compassion demonstrated by the two agents. Very much a departure from what I see as standard operating procedure.

As for the scip itself, I could have gone for a bit more exploration into its effects, both externally and internally. But, beyond that, you more than earned my +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It covers a very large area making the physical difficulty of containment high. It's tough to wall off a 5 sq km area that roads go through / used to go through and have people not notice. That's the reason it's keter rather than the anomaly itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice one, +1, but I don't see why this should be keter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yikes, that was a heavy dose of the feels.

While I feel you do play your hand as to what is going on and explain the mystery a little early, that isn't all that much a detriment to the piece. Honestly, the mystery aside, this is some excellent character work and the details that show just how ingrained in Lorum Ipsum the whole town is are a very nice touch.

Not much else to say really. A solid article and a great addition to the contest.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I appreciate your kind words! Thank you, reader.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Absolutely fantastic. If I love this, it's because I had a similar idea once upon a time, but this is so much better in the concept and execution. Bravo!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's true there's not a whole lot of mystery, and I would have liked to see the relationship between Joe and Daniel developed more, but overall this is very good. It feels like an old-school Twilight Zone episode.

item.title: Major updates
item.content:encoded:
Per some of the feedback I've received and after further consideration, I've removed the interpretive dreaming bits and mushed some of that into another tape. I further massaged the ending interview to reflect these changes while keeping the flow of information and the "final message" intact.

Thank for your feedback, dudes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"After the ball hits '1' and 'Happy New Year' is shouter, the TV cuts to an announcer who begins speaking lorem ipsum. The camera is manually turned off."

that was absolutely chilling. seriously, I felt like my stomach with filled with ice. O.o I'm surprised a trained agent wouldn't have found that disconcerting. well, I can, actually. he wanted to believe that dude was ready to move on so he ignored a relatively small red flag.

also, I think you mean "shouted" there. :-)

I really loved this. I loved babadook for being a horror movie that was really about grief, the monster that can consume us unless we learn how to see beyond it without necessarily rebuking it altogether. I know for sure part of my enjoyment is sentimental. as a parent… I don't fully understand the lengths I'd go to for my children. but the biggest risks I've taken in my life, I took for them.

it feels really apropos that I was listening to the marianas trench album "phantoms" while i read this, an album that's about loss and longing and being haunted by what we've lost.

easy +1 for me.

item.title: Junior Staff - Site Crit
item.content:encoded:
As someone who generally doesn't get that much emotion from reading, this did make me feel something. The dialogue and most of the events were pretty realistic, and overall this is worthy of my upvote. I actually enjoyed the anticlimax and lack of any happy or even especially bittersweet resolution, the note this ended on was much more impactful. There's two pieces of feedback I will offer, however:

  • The quotation marks surrounding dialogue seem unnecessary. They're generally not present in logs on the site as far as I'm aware.
  • I'll second psul's view that the interpretive dreaming record was a little too big of a leap in technology. While there were some advanced-sounding temporal things, the anomaly is a temporal one and they fitted in a little better.

Overall though, I liked this. It's a good one, even if the mystery aspect isn't really that much of the focus.

Disclaimer: Opinions expressed in this review do not reflect the opinions of SCP Wiki staff as a whole. Please respond to gee0765 or contact the captain of the Site Criticism Team, SoullessSingularity, for any questions or concerns relating to this review.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for your feedback! "Christmas is over" depression is a personal tie-in to this story I very much experience myself, so I am glad someone else was able to observe and comment on this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for your feedback; it is admittedly quite light on mystery, and the emotions being explored are the "main point" rather than anything else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
exploration logs aren't really interesting when I already know what is happening and who is responsible for it.

I can't really fault that you don't find them interesting under these circumstances. I chose to present like this so I could focus on the underlying emotion of the story rather than the essence of it being a mystery.

Also, I don't see a reason for including chroniton particles.

They are the excuse as to why people cannot come and go and why we cannot just load up this location with cameras and watch away. They provide a vehicle for limited observation that restricts POV.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really enjoyed this. The themes of an inability to move on from grief are very powerful, and this captured them perfectly. It also comes on the heels of my annual "Sad that Christmas is over "feelings. All in all I really enjoyed this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I like the SCP, and find it to be worth a read and a +1, I don't really feel like it embodies much of a mystery at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My biggest gripe with this article is that the description spoils pretty much everything about the "mystery": exploration logs aren't really interesting when I already know what is happening and who is responsible for it. The only question left is why, which is also answered pretty quickly. On the other hand, the parts of the scip that I found interesting (lorem ipsum and other gibberish replacing parts of speech; Joe convincing the entire town to give gifts to his son) are rather short.

Also, I don't see a reason for including chroniton particles. I initially thought they were going to be important to the story, but they are only mentioned once after the introduction to explain the agents' death (which doesn't seem to serve any purpose). I don't think anything would be lost if the chronitons were scrapped altogether and the tapes were replaced with the agents' field notes. It's not like the damage to tapes is important: the various "The rest of the tape is unsalvageable" don't really add any suspence and the exploration log already reads like a whole coherent story anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It’s good! I don’t think it’s quite SCP-5000 good, but I really enjoyed the story you told with it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I appreciate your feedback! I'd clarify that it was the agent's decision and word choice to call it word salad and not necessarily the clinically accurate description of what was going on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really liked this one! Leaves me with a strange feeling of sadness, and I really had Silent Hill-vibes if Silent Hill wasn't the hellish town it is, idk if it's clear haha
In any case, +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very nice idea, but the story doesn't so much climax as peter out. Perhaps Joe could realise one morning that he can't remember his dead wife's face anymore - something that makes him doubt his strategy is ever going to work. Or, he's trying to reminisce about a time he talked with his son, but he can't recall anything the boy ever said before the accident.

Small detail, but I'm not sure about using "lorem ipsum" in a formal document to describe word salad. Could use "grammatically valid but meaningless sentences", "unconnected dialog fragments", or "nonsensical utterances".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A great article. Maybe not worthy of being SCP-5000, but I really liked it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you very much for the spelling/grammar help, it's always appreciated. You are very right about shearing vs sheering.

I also appreciate your thoughts on the dreaming matrix versus another video, and I'll consider how I might handle that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is pretty good (I really liked the generically named products, and 685 December), but ultimately felt a little too explicit/explain-y for my liking. We learn pretty early on that this is Joe's inability deal with grief, and the story doesn't really escalate much from there. The interpretive dreaming record seemed a big leap from a believability perspective, especially when most of that information was covered elsewhere - it might have been better to put all this into another video log, to make it shown not told.

Some spelling notes: I think "sheering" should be "shearing", and there's an "Anway" in the final log that should be "Anyway", as well as a "he had no dwelt" which should be "not dwelt", unless Dureau is Scottish ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agent Dureau reminds me of Agent Cooper from Twin Peaks

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
A December To Remember

My exploration of the theme "mystery" comes in the small town of Elk Grove, Wisconsin where two agents attempt to unravel why time has frozen in December of 1979. I hope you enjoy this exploration of the price of grief.

Big thanks to Soulless Singularity for their critical review, as well as Stormbreath

EDIT: Additional thanks to Psul and Gee0765 for their post-posting crit which lead me to make a major update to the end of this article.

Image:
"Day 21. Small town life." by davidmulder61 is licensed under CC BY-SA 2.0
